description: Fastify-based API Gateway that proxies requests to three microservices: Users, Groups, and Tickets. Handles authentication, authorization, and request routing.

architecture.authenticationMechanism: JWT (via Authorization header or jwt cookie)
authenticationFlow: JWT tokens sent via Authorization: Bearer {token} header OR jwt cookie
Gateway validates tokens using app.authenticate() before proxying
Permissions checked at gateway level via app.requirePermission()
Some endpoints delegate authorization to microservices (e.g., ownership rules)

responseStandard.shape: { statusCode: number, intOpCode: string, message: string, data: array }

ticketPriority.note: Frontend sends priority as string (Low/Medium/High), backend stores as numeric (1/2/3)

endpoints.description: Health check endpoint to verify that the API Gateway is running and responding.
endpoints.permissionNotes: No authentication or permissions required. Public endpoint.
businessRules: Returns 200 OK if gateway is running
Useful for load balancers and monitoring
frontendNotes: Use for connectivity checks
No authentication needed
Can poll this endpoint to check gateway availability
inferenceNotes: OpCode SGWHL200 = Success Gateway Health 200
Defined directly in app.ts
Does not proxy to any microservice

endpoints.description: Authenticates a user through the Users service.
endpoints.permissionNotes: No authentication or permissions required for login.
validationRules: email must be a valid email format
password is required but no specific format validation is shown in gateway

responses.whenItHappens: When credentials are valid and user is authenticated successfully
When email or password is incorrect

businessRules: Proxies request to Users microservice at /api/User/login
Returns JWT token with expiration date and user permissions
Token should be stored by frontend for subsequent authenticated requests
Permissions array indicates what actions the user can perform
frontendNotes: Store the returned token in localStorage or secure cookie
Store permissions array to control UI visibility
Use token in Authorization header as 'Bearer {token}' for subsequent requests
Also supported: send token in 'jwt' cookie
Handle 401 error with user-friendly message about invalid credentials
Redirect to home/dashboard after successful login
inferenceNotes: Response structure follows standard: {statusCode, intOpCode, message, data[]}
Data is always an array, even for single object responses
Actual authentication logic is in Users microservice
OpCode SUSLG200 = Success User Service Login 200
OpCode EUSAU401 = Error User Service Authentication Unauthorized 401

endpoints.description: Logs out the current authenticated user.
endpoints.permissionNotes: Requires valid JWT token but no specific permissions. Authentication is verified via app.authenticate() preHandler.

cookies.name: jwt
cookies.description: Alternative to Authorization header. JWT token can be sent via cookie.

validationRules: Valid JWT token required in Authorization header or jwt cookie
Token must be signed with JWT_SECRET_KEY
Token must have valid issuer and audience

responses.whenItHappens: When user is authenticated and logout is processed successfully
When no token is provided in Authorization header or jwt cookie
When token is malformed, expired, or signature verification fails

businessRules: Proxies request to Users microservice at /api/User/logout
Token validation happens in gateway before proxying
Actual logout logic (token invalidation) happens in Users microservice
Frontend should clear stored token after successful logout
frontendNotes: Clear stored token from localStorage/cookies after successful logout
Clear stored user permissions
Redirect to login page after logout
Handle 401 errors by redirecting to login
No request body needed for this endpoint
inferenceNotes: OpCode SUSLO200 = Success User Service Logout 200
OpCode EGWAU401 = Error Gateway Authentication Unauthorized 401
Token can be sent via Authorization header OR jwt cookie
Gateway validates token before proxying to microservice

endpoints.description: Retrieves the list of users.
endpoints.permissionNotes: Requires canRead_Users permission. Validated in gateway via app.requirePermission().

responses.whenItHappens: When user is authenticated and has canRead_Users permission

responses.message: You do not have permission to perform this action.
responses.whenItHappens: When user is authenticated but doesn't have canRead_Users permission

responseExample.message: You do not have permission to perform this action.

businessRules: Returns all users in the system
Each user includes their permissions array
frontendNotes: Use this endpoint to populate users list/table
Display firstName, middleName, lastName, email, userName
permissions array useful for showing user roles/capabilities
Handle middleName as optional (can be null)
Show 403 error when user lacks permission to view users list
inferenceNotes: OpCode SUSRD200 = Success User Service Read 200
Returns array of user objects
Each user has permissions array

endpoints.description: Retrieves the currently authenticated user.
endpoints.permissionNotes: Requires authentication but no specific permissions. Any authenticated user can retrieve their own data.
ownershipRules: User can only retrieve their own data based on JWT token

responses.whenItHappens: When token is invalid, expired, or malformed

businessRules: Returns authenticated user's data based on JWT userId claim
Does not return permissions array (unlike get-all endpoint)
frontendNotes: Use this to display current user profile
Call after login to get user details
Does NOT include permissions (use login response for permissions)
Useful for profile page, navbar user display
Handle 401 by redirecting to login
inferenceNotes: OpCode SUSME200 = Success User Service Me 200
Returns single user in array
User ID extracted from JWT token

endpoints.description: Retrieves a specific user by id.
endpoints.permissionNotes: Requires canRead_Users permission.
validationRules: userId must be a valid UUID string

responses.whenItHappens: When user exists and requester has canRead_Users permission
When userId doesn't exist in database

businessRules: Returns specific user with permissions array
frontendNotes: Use for user detail modal/page
Display full user info including permissions
Handle 404 with user-friendly 'User not found' message
Permissions array shows what the user can do in the system
inferenceNotes: OpCode EUSNF404 = Error User Service Not Found 404
Returns single user in array format

endpoints.description: Creates a new user.
endpoints.permissionNotes: Requires canCreate_Users permission.

email must be unique in system
all required fields must be provided

responses.whenItHappens: When required fields are missing or data format is invalid

responseExample.message: A user with the same email already exists.

businessRules: Password validation rules enforced by microservice
frontendNotes: Use returned ID to redirect to user detail or refresh users list
Show success message after 201
Handle 409 with specific message about duplicate email
Handle 400 with field validation errors
Refresh users list after successful creation
middleName field is optional in form
inferenceNotes: OpCode SUSCR201 = Success User Service Create 201
OpCode EUSBR400 = Error User Service Bad Request 400
OpCode EUSCF409 = Error User Service Conflict 409
Returns only the new user's ID

endpoints.description: Updates a user. This operation is available to privileged users or to the same authenticated user depending on backend rules.
endpoints.permissionNotes: Requires authentication. Permission check delegated to microservice based on ownership rules.
ownershipRules: User can update their own profile (inferred)
Users with canUpdate_Users permission can update any user (inferred)
Actual authorization logic in microservice
validationRules: email must be valid email format if provided
at least one field should be provided to update (inferred)

responses.message: You are not authorized to update this user.
responses.whenItHappens: When user tries to update another user without permission

responseExample.message: You are not authorized to update this user.

businessRules: Privileged users can update any user (inferred from description)
frontendNotes: Use for user profile edit form
All fields are optional (send only changed fields)
Handle 403 with clear authorization error message
Refresh user data after successful update
For editing own profile, use /users/me to get current data first
inferenceNotes: OpCode SUSUP200 = Success User Service Update 200
OpCode EUSFB403 = Error User Service Forbidden 403
No gateway-level permission check, delegated to microservice
All fields are optional in schema

endpoints.description: Updates the permissions of a specific user.
endpoints.permissionNotes: Requires canUpdate_Users permission.

routeParams.name: userId
routeParams.description: User ID to update permissions (UUID format)

permissionIds.description: Array of permission IDs to assign to user

validationRules: permissionIds must be array of valid UUID strings (inferred)
businessRules: Replaces user's permissions with provided array
Get available permissions from /users/permissions endpoint first
frontendNotes: Use /users/permissions to get list of available permissions first
Show multi-select or checkbox list of permissions
Send array of permission IDs
Refresh user data after update to see new permissions
This is typically admin-only functionality
inferenceNotes: OpCode SUSPM200 = Success User Service Permissions 200
Likely replaces all permissions (not additive - inferred)
Frontend should fetch current permissions before update

endpoints.description: Changes the password of a user. This operation is available to privileged users or to the same authenticated user depending on backend rules.

ownershipRules: User can change their own password (must provide currentPassword)

currentPassword.description: Current password (required when user changes own password)

validationRules: currentPassword required when user changes own password (enforced by microservice)

responses.message: You are not authorized to update this password.
responses.whenItHappens: When user tries to change another user's password without permission

responseExample.message: You are not authorized to update this password.

responses.whenItHappens: When currentPassword doesn't match user's actual current password

businessRules: User changing own password must provide currentPassword
Admin/privileged users may change password without currentPassword (inferred)
Does not return any data, only status
frontendNotes: For user changing own password: require currentPassword field
Handle 409 with message about incorrect current password
Show success message after 200
Consider forcing re-login after password change
Validate newPassword strength on frontend before sending
inferenceNotes: OpCode SUSPW200 = Success User Service Password 200
currentPassword is nullable, enforcement logic in microservice
No data returned on success

endpoints.description: Retrieves the full list of available permissions.

businessRules: Returns all available permissions in system
frontendNotes: Use this to populate permission selection UI

detectedInconsistencies.description: Frontend sends priority as string ('Low', 'Medium', 'High') but backend likely stores as numeric (1, 2, 3). Gateway schema shows enum as strings.
detectedInconsistencies.recommendation: Clarify with backend team if conversion happens in microservice or if both formats are accepted

detectedInconsistencies.issue: Status field meaning varies by context
detectedInconsistencies.description: User status and Group status both use 0/1 (Inactive/Active), but Ticket status uses 1/2/3 (Open/InProgress/Closed). May cause confusion.
detectedInconsistencies.recommendation: Consider using consistent status naming across entities or documenting clearly

detectedInconsistencies.description: The /users/:userId/status endpoint uses DELETE method but requires a request body with status field. This is unusual as DELETE typically has no body.
detectedInconsistencies.recommendation: Consider using PATCH method instead, or make status a query parameter

detectedInconsistencies.description: Groups add member and remove member both return SGRMB200, making it harder to distinguish operations in logs.
detectedInconsistencies.recommendation: Consider using distinct OpCodes like SGRAM200 (add) and SGRRM200 (remove)

detectedInconsistencies.description: Tickets have both 'description' and 'comments' fields. The distinction and use case for each is not clearly documented.
detectedInconsistencies.recommendation: Clarify: description = initial problem statement, comments = additional notes/updates?
